one_line_takeaway: Across 521 LLM-driven binary vulnerability-analysis sessions (99,563 steps), the authors empirically extract and characterize four recurring, structurally distinct, temporally organized 'implicit' reasoning patterns -- early pruning, path lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization -- showing LLM agents self-organize long-horizon exploration via token-level dynamics rather than ad hoc or externally imposed control logic.
key_finding: Patterns are highly prevalent and non-uniform: P2 (Lock-in) and P4 (Prioritization) each appear in 97.6% of sessions (509/521), P3 (Backtracking) in 93.8% (489/521), P1 (Pruning) in 83.5% (435/521). Densities differ sharply: P4 averages 27.03 instances/session (27.71 per active session, max 278 -- extreme bursts), P2 averages 18.53 (max 77), P1 averages 6.41 (max 44), P3 averages only 1.91 (max 5, a hard-capped 'limited recovery' role). Temporally, P2 is early-biased (24.0% of its instances in the first 10% of session progress), P1 peaks mid-session (12.9% at phase bin 6), and P3 is strongly late-biased (46.5% of instances in the final 10% of the session). Transition analysis over block-collapsed pattern sequences shows P2<->P1 form a bidirectional loop (P2->P1: 40.0%/1,947 transitions; P1->P2: 39.4%/1,918 transitions), together accounting for 79.4% of all pattern switches, with alternating subsequences (P2->P1->P2->P1, P1->P2->P1->P2) among the most frequent length-3/4 subsequences (1,508 and 1,488 occurrences respectively). P3 transitions mainly to P4 (10.1%, 492 instances of P3->P4), which then commonly leads to P2 (9.6%, 467 instances of P4->P2), forming a 'recovery-to-prioritization-to-commitment' macro-structure (P3->P4->P2: 451 occurrences). P2 and P4 show a strong negative correlation in normalized per-session usage (r = -0.845: P4 averages 0.377 when P2 is above-median, 0.566 when P2 is below-median), interpreted as complementary resource allocation. Behaviorally, P1 has the longest/most variable paths (mean L=19.9, median 9.0) with high forward commitment and minimal backtracking; P2 has short stable paths (median L=7.0, median forward ratio F=1.0) with almost no backtracking; P3 is defined by exactly one backtrack per instance (mean/median R=1.0, std 0.000) and short paths (mean L=5.3); P4 has consistently short paths (mean L=6.0, std only 0.6). Of the 521 sessions, 198 (38.0%) surfaced at least one CWE-labeled vulnerability finding, totaling 306 distinct vulnerability instances across the corpus -- reported only to establish the sessions involved non-trivial security tasks, not as a performance/accuracy result.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is the first large-scale, trace-level empirical study characterizing how LLM agents (acting as sole decision-maker, with no external search algorithm) organize long-horizon exploration when performing iterative, tool-driven binary vulnerability analysis. From 521 analysis sessions (99,563 reasoning steps total) over 521 real-world ARM/MIPS ELF32 firmware binaries, the authors define and extract four recurring 'token-level implicit patterns' via a rule-based detector operating on trace structure (revisits, path divergence, persistence): P1 Early Pruning of Candidate Paths, P2 Path-Dependent Analysis Lock-in, P3 Multi-Path Exploration with Targeted Backtracking, and P4 Knowledge-Guided Prioritization. They then quantify (RQ1) prevalence/density of each pattern per session, (RQ2) transition structure between patterns (block-level Markov-style transitions and recurring subsequences), and (RQ3) pattern-conditioned behavioral metrics (path length, branching factor, forward-step ratio, backtrack count) computed independently of the extraction rules as external validation. This is explicitly framed as a descriptive/characterization study, not a new detection technique, defense, or attack, and the authors explicitly disclaim any causal or performance-predictive claim linking pattern usage to vulnerability-discovery success.
threat_model: None proposed — this is not an attack or defense paper. The 'security' content is the domain of the object under study (LLM agents performing binary vulnerability analysis for taint-style bugs such as command injection and path traversal in firmware binaries), not a threat model against a system. No adversary, attack surface, or defense mechanism is defined; the paper studies benign agent reasoning behavior during legitimate vulnerability-hunting sessions.
limitations: The paper explicitly limits its own scope: it does not evaluate vulnerability-detection accuracy, coverage, or agent performance, and explicitly states there is no claimed causal relationship between pattern frequency and vulnerability-discovery outcomes. Pattern extraction relies on rule-based structural proxies (heuristics over revisits/path divergence/persistence) rather than a formally validated ground-truth labeling of 'implicit patterns,' so the taxonomy's construct validity rests on the authors' rule design (only partially mitigated by the independent RQ3 metric validation). One representative session is selected per binary per model-set (chosen for completeness/length) rather than analyzing all 521x4 sessions jointly, discarding most of the multi-LLM session data for the primary analysis. The corpus is restricted to ELF32 firmware binaries with taint-style vulnerabilities (command injection, path traversal) pre-filtered by Mango, so generalization to other binary formats, architectures, or vulnerability classes (e.g., memory corruption without taint sinks) is untested. The study also does not report which of the four LLMs contributes to which pattern statistics or whether pattern prevalence/behavior differs materially across models, despite using four heterogeneous LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, GPT-5, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 3.0) to generate sessions.

datasets_used: Karonte firmware dataset (source of 30,195 Linux-based firmware binaries)
Custom corpus of 521 deduplicated ELF32 firmware binaries (ARM 328 / MIPS 193; screened via Mango for taint-style vulnerabilities)
99,563-step reasoning-trace dataset generated by the authors from 521 LLM-agent analysis sessions (radare2/Ghidra via r2ghidra tool interface)
quantitative_results: 521 binaries, 99,563 total reasoning steps, median 160.0 / mean 191.1 (+/-154.9) steps per session
Pattern density (avg/active session): P1 7.68, P2 18.99, P3 2.03, P4 27.71 (P4 max burst = 278, P3 max = 5)
Temporal bias: P2 24.0% of instances in first phase bin; P1 peaks 12.9% at phase bin 6; P3 46.5% of instances in final phase bin
P2 vs P4 normalized-count correlation r = -0.845 (P4 averages 0.377 when P2 above median vs 0.566 when below)

Behavioral metrics (mean/median): P1 path length 19.9/9.0, forward ratio 0.869/0.947; P2 path length 8.9/7.0, forward ratio 0.944/1.0; P3 path length 5.3/3.0, backtrack count 1.0/1.0 (std 0.000); P4 path length 6.0/6.0 (std 0.6)
38.0% of sessions (198/521) identified at least one CWE-labeled vulnerability finding; 306 distinct vulnerability instances total across the corpus
baselines_compared: No detection/defense baselines are compared; this is a descriptive/characterization study, not a benchmarked method paper. The only 'comparison' axis is across four LLM backends used to generate sessions (DeepSeek-V3, GPT-5, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 3.0), and the paper states pattern distributions are similar across architectures, LLM models, and compilation settings without reporting per-model breakdown tables in the main text portion reviewed.
